speaker: interviewer
text: September, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling me how you brought your wife to where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the second trial, as I told you, came to nothing. Then I met, in Warsaw, she found me a cousin of mine who's husband was senator of Poland, a member of Polish senate. And during the First World War, he was one of the principles of the Polish underground army. He'd died. He had a heart attack in the start of the-- at the beginning of the war. I saw him in Lviv. Anyway, he was a very bright guy, and he was a head of a group in, before the Second World War, of a Polish group, leftist group-- not a socialist group, so-called [POLISH], a part of town. And under him, was in his group the later the prime minister of Poland, the deputy president of-- and the vice president of Poland, a number of others. And with her, we went to see this vice president of Poland, Schwalbe was his name. Seeing him were two reasons-- one was to arrange something for my wife, to get her out of Russia. Second one, we, together with them, had a farm, a large farm, a holiday place, as well, not far from Lodz. And this was taken over by the government. And we wanted to-- to get it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we approached this vice president of Poland. And he promised that he will do something about it. It was later returned, but then he was nationalist again, as I found out later, much later. Anyway, he called immediately the foreign minister, and while we were there-- Modelevsky was his name-- and he told him to take immediate steps to get my wife out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came with him. Next day, I was called in and I was told that he found out that the previous day in Moscow was signed a pact between Poland-- Hitler-- that's what I mean, he didn't know about it. In Russia, that all the individual migration cases are stopped and only will be organized migrations. So my wife could not get out. She later came out illegally with falsified documents. As a matter of fact, it was hard for her. She got documents that she was a wife of a Polish officer. As a matter of fact, she got documents she was a wife of a Polish general, which I never was. [LAUGHS] But we falsified documents for her this time. And she came. She was in Poland two weeks. I was, by this time, already released from the army. And we escaped Poland in a army truck, Russian army truck, which was delivering mail to the Russian units, between Poland, Czechoslovakia, and Vienna, the Russian part of Vienna. And we got into the truck between the mail and the front of the truck, with the officer sitting in front next of the driver, and a soldier with a rifle sitting in the back. And we got through all the borders and finish in the American zone of Vienna. And this how I got out of Poland. From there--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- did you ever, at any stretch, find out exactly what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found out already in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found out that my father died from a heart attack. He had pneumonia and then he got a heart attack and he died in 1942. And my mother was taken to Treblinka in 1943. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I found out, as well, during this period, how the Poles behaved. I found out that when the ghetto was burning, they had, like Luna Park here-- a big wheel. So the Poles were going-- it was Easter-- were going on this wheel to look how the Jews are burning. I was told by a friend of mine, who was younger, was to school with me. He was-- officially he was Polish, he was half Jewish. And he was in the high command of the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] underground army, was called [POLISH]. And he told me how the Poles were denouncing the Jews, how they were shooting them, even during the uprising. The Jews were with them together in the uprising against Germans, and they were shot in the back. This was the confirmed to me by others later. Look, to me, it was a colossal shock because I was very assimilated. I considered myself a Pole, until the certain time. And it was-- I never want to go to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway-- So you were telling me about getting into Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got into Vienna. We were a few days in Vienna. And we left already in sealed cars through the Russian territory, into Germany, into the American zone of Germany. When we arrived in Germany, Munich was destroyed. I went to Steinberg am See, and it looked very nice. It was a beautiful place. And I got, from Munich, positioned at palace home there. And this where we moved in. In fact, this was not far from the-- all around were plenty of Jewish displaced person camps. And I went to Feldafing, where there was number of people from the same region where I came from. [DRINKS] And then I started working as a civilian employee for UNRRA. UNRRA was the United Nations Refugee and Relief Agency. It was taking care of displaced persons. And after short while, they offered me a job, a uniformed job, under contract. I started as a supply officer, I finished as a legal adviser, with the same right and privileges as a full colonel in American army. And I had a very good life. I was-- you had parties with the military government, officers-- I mean, they were-- I had a beautiful home with a swimming pool in the forest, you know. And private car and chauffeur, duty car and driver. A very great life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were some of your task in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Assisting in the courts where displaced person were involved or where it was some-- German stories, as a friend of court. Taking care about different problem-- Jewish problem, problems of the inmates of the-- former inmates of the camps. I was pretty busy with this. Plenty-- the American style conferences all around Germany, you know, going from one place to another, driving hundreds of kilometers for two hours' meeting. Going back, you never knew why you came, you knew never knew why you came back. I made plenty of friends in UNRRA, which were later became IRO, International Refuge Organization. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But the problem was this was a fool's paradise. Everybody was telling me that I should go to the United States. And this was the dream of everybody in Europe, how to go to United States. And I was in a special privilege position because I was born in Germany. The quotas to United States for immigration were not according to your nationality, or citizenship, but according to place of birth. And they were established in the beginning of the century, according to the percentage of migrants then already being in United States. And the Germans had a quota of 26,000. But the Polish quota was 2 and 1/2 thousand. It was no German could go because they were the enemy aliens. And it was not-- very few German Jews who-- so the quota was open for me to go tomorrow. And I decided to go to Australia, I was waiting two and a half years for my permits. Now I will tell you the story of the permits to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we get to that, just who was some of your heads while you were in UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was some of the people that were involved that--

speaker: interviewee
text: UNRRA? La Guardia was the head for Europe. [DRINKS] After La Guardia retired, it was General Morgan, who was an English general, General Morgan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My immediate? Oh, this is an interesting story too. It was a lieutenant colonel of the army, a Jew, Rabinovich. Hard drinking, big guy. I'd say it was the team lived in Gauting, in the house of Professor Junkers-- you know, the constructor of the German planes. Very beautiful place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we had parties very often there. And was one party, and somewhere came a big discussion about Jews who took part in the committees inside the ghettos, you know? Distributing clothes, doing any-- all-- this was-- they were all autonomous organizations under the Germans in the ghettos. And he made very strong statement that these people should be shot, and should be this, and they were criminals.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was never under the Germans. I was not an interested party. But I-- at one moment, I couldn't listen to the stupidity any more. And I went to him and I said, look, before you talk, you have to know what you are talking about it. When you were sitting in New York and having good time, these people were dying. And without any organization, they would be all gone. So don't talk about it. And he got so cranky with me, and was dissatisfied. The next day, he's going special courier to the headquarters for Europe for UNRRA to discharge me. And the next day, he went to the swimming pool and got a heart attack and died. And I immediately arranged for somebody else to take over provisionally his job, because I was frightened of his girlfriend who was administrative officer. She was an English girl. And she was-- she knew this story. She was very much against me, too. But anyway, she was kicked out of UNRRA. [LAUGHS] Then the next fellow, this was interesting thing. We were under-- all our supply and everything was coming under special department of military government. And the head of the department was American colonel, Decker, who came from Texas, very nice guy. He was extremely helpful. I was always in contact with him. And, one day, we got the information there is a new director coming, but that he's a mulatto. Beaufort was his name, Lincoln Beaufort. And when I went to Munich to see Decker, I told him this. And he became white, like this sheet of paper. And he said to me, Manny, from now on, any contacts between your headquarters and our headquarters by telephone or letter. I knows that it's wrong, but this is in my blood-- I don't deal with niggers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he never came close. He was a very nice guy, otherwise. And one day came a major in connection with some building stories-- he was Jewish, an engineer. And I was-- he saw me, and I was-- I knew him and I was showing to him to our headquarters. And I open the door of Beaufort's office. And I opened the door, and he saw the negro. He was already in the room. He turned back and walked out. I couldn't look Beaufort in his eyes. Beaufort was an extremely intelligent, a polyglot, speaking about seven or eight languages well-- with colossal education. This was a very high position. It was a position of a general, lieutenant general, I think. Equivalent, it was. For the negro to get this position, a mulatto, he had to be 20 times as good as a white. And I couldn't look-- when this major walked out, I couldn't look Beaufort in his eyes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Beaufort was a very able guy, very at this. Look, I was in Germany when I was waiting for the migration papers. Finally, in December, before Christmas, '47, I got the telegram that the papers are coming. What happened in the meantime, what I knew about it, the position was beside individual permits were called people as a displaced persons going on contract. But we notice that they don't accept any Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After a while, we knew that this is not coincidental. Then I found out what happened. In the United States was a so-called Jewish Labor Committee. I don't know if you ever heard about this. The head of the Jewish Labor Committee was Dubinsky, who was the president of the International Government Workers Association, and senior vice president of American Federation of labor. The first minister from immigration from Australia was Caldwell. When Caldwell went first time for overseas trip, his first stop was in United States. And he got audience with Green, who was the president of the American Federation of Labor. And during this meeting, Dubinsky was present. Dubinsky later told me the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Dubinsky attacked immediately Caldwell, and told him, look, you are a socialist? You don't accept Jews into this? And Caldwell told him that is a lie. And he said, look, he said, it's not a lie, because we have the information from Germany-- we know it. He said give me a list of 100 Jewish families, and I will see that they come on this list. So they sent to us-- to Germany for the list, and I was put as number one, on top of the list. In the mean time, I got the permits that I never used it. But I am only telling you how the situation was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, I remember we had a party in Junkers' house for the general director of UNRRA for Europe. I forgot his name. He was, during the war, the general director of the American Red Cross. And later, after the war, he was general director of UNRRA for Czechoslovakia. Czechoslovakia. And when Masaryk was-- when Jan Maseryk, Maseryk was killed, the foreign minister, thrown out through the window, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he was the director there. He gave his papers, and the papers of few of his staff, to a number of people-- Jews, mostly-- and they escaped Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: This, few months later, came out and the Czechoslovakian government started protests in Washington. And he was recalled from Europe. So it was a party for him before he was going. And I was sitting at of the garden party with the military governor for the region, and his girlfriend, and my wife. And he came to me and asking, Manny, what is going on with you? When are we leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, as a matter of fact, I have the permits. Next week is coming, the officer from the Australian Military Mission from Berlin. He's the only person who's entitled to give visas. So  he said, oh, so you are going. I said, no, I'm not going, because I know that he doesn't have right to give visas to Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, oh, and how will he know that you are Jewish? I said, because I will tell him. He said, why? I said, because I don't want to start my life in a new country on a lie.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said you are stupid. The country's immoral if they put such conditions. Don't tell him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought, maybe he's right-- the guy is right. And so when we drove with my wife back to our home in Steinberg. I said to her, look, when we go next Wednesday, he ask this, answer that, he ask this, answer that. God knows what. And we don't admit that we are Jewish. So really, we went on Wednesday morning, I was in the American officer's uniform, in all that. And I was first taken in.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very nice guy, from Sydney. And he talked me, asked me where my sponsors live. And I said I don't know. I don't know Sydney, but I had the address Bearview Hill. He said, oh, it's a very beautiful part of Sydney. And he said, oh, I hope you will do well in Australia and give all of you my best wishes to you. And he took the seal to put it on my envelope.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as he was going down, he said, you aren't Jewish, Mr. Darin, are you? And I said, yes, I am. And they guy became as white as this paper. And he said to me, Mr. Darin, I don't have the right to give you the visa, but the right is only given to the consul general in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sending a special courier. If within 48 hours I don't have a visa for you, I know who is going to Australia. I am leaving. I am resigning my job. And in 48 hours, I had the visa. I'm telling only about-- how it were, the conditions. They were-- I came by boat from Marseilles. This was in August, '48. I still came to the train in Munich in my army Jeep. [LAUGHS] And all the team was there to say good bye to me, Beaufort, and all the other members of the team. And I left, I charge the boat in Marseilles. It was the worse boat which was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] available, I think. It was belonged to a Greek guy who was the father-in-law of Onassis Niarchos. Dirty [INAUDIBLE]. This was a boy taken by the Allies during the First World War from Germans. It was a freighter-- was a freighter before the World War. And this boat was going, it took as along as Captain Cook to come to Australia. [LAUGHS] Really, 10 weeks from Marseilles. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When we came on the boat, there was UNRRA representative, English guy, actually from Canada, highly anti-Semitic. And he started-- they were on the boat, Jews, and Latvians, and Estonians, and what have you, all mixture of different nationalities. The condition was for all the boats and planes that they should not take more than 10% of Jews, but this was-- for example, there was going a group of children, waifs, sent by HIAS-- and officially, they were going as non-Jewish from Paris. You know, to cover the 10% problem. Because there was much more than 10% percent of Jews on the boat. Once we arrived in Australia, the problem was over. I never heard about the Jewish, non-Jewish problem in Australia. But on the boat, this guy was making plenty of intrigues. And he knows that I was on high position before in UNRRA. And I told him once off and it didn't help. And when we arrived in Melbourne-- because this was the end, where the ship finished-- he came to me and I want to say goodbye. And I told him, look, piss off. And I told him, look, I will see to it that you'll never work for IRO again. And, really, I wrote a report to the headquarters of IRO, to people who I knew. And I was later informed that he was sent on the first available plane to Canada. He never worked. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I am coming to the period in Australia. I have to say, there is one thing for sure-- it was not a day from my arrival in Australia that I regretted it. The first period when I arrived here, I worked as a laborer for half a year. I had some money. I had some money to stay, but I want to learn the conditions here before doing anything. I didn't have much money, but I had some. Then after a half a year, my wife, in Germany, I-- because I was busy all the time and going from one meeting in one part of Germany to another meeting in another part of Germany-- and my wife was supposed to learn-- HIAS had courses, learn how to make shirts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought that we decided to make shirts, with her as a specialist. And I found out that, unfortunately, she didn't learn very much. [LAUGHS] Pardon me. I bought a little workshop with this money I had. This was a tragedy-- it was all gone. And I bought in partnership with a guy. It was awful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came the big strike. It was a coal mine strike, I don't know if you remember. That was in '49, a big coal mine strike, which closed all the industry, in New South Wales. Of course, no electricity, nothing. And we had a small, coincidentally, motor over there-- diesel, so we could run the bench and one light.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this help us, because we got a contract. Anyway, I was for 10 years in the clothing industry. We start to make later frocks. We were quite successful, but I hated every day of my work. I hated the people with whom I had to deal, doesn't matter who they were, small retailers, buyers from miles, [INAUDIBLE]-- God knows what is-- hated everything about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, one day, I came to my partner. I said, all right, here, you have it. I am out. You don't have to pay me any good will, 50% of the assets and I am going. And I went and I started from scratch in my car for the chemical, with a friend who came from Poland. And it worked out. It became a very large enterprise. And I made well with it. And this was that. I can only say that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Australia was very good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on your experiences through the war years, what do you think you learned?

speaker: interviewee
text: I completely-- I became a different person to the person I was before the outbreak of the war. I learned another philosophy of life. This what I learned-- another approach. Other values. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Very hard to describe what I learned. It was nothing-- it was-- generally, what I learned, as I say again, it was a philosophy of life, approach to life. I learned it, yes. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Look, I could tell you many more interesting stories of this period. I try to cut back on it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I want-- yes, the one thing what I want to add, the story with my car. While I was in-- when I was in the Polish army, in Poland, before I got discharged, I saw one day in the paper that a new representative of the British government arrived in Warsaw and that he was the previous consul in Katowice. So I went to see him in the hotel where he was provisionally staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came-- I had his name, Holiday, and I couldn't recall then-- I said, what's the name whom I knew? But I went to see him. It was a guy who I never knew. I was in uniform still. And I ask him have you been to the consul in Katowice? He said, yes. I said, look, I remember someone else. He said, yes, I moved out of Katowice few months before the outbreak of the war, and I'm Mr. Twades took over from me. I said, yes, this is the name I remember. Where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he says he was consul during the war in Chicago. And then he was working for the Ministry of Information. So if you tried to contact the foreign ministry, the record office, you might get in touch with him. So I really, when I found myself in Germany, in Steinberg. I wrote. And after a while, I got the reply that Mr. Twades will contact me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And two or three months later, I receive a letter in an envelope of the British Consulate General from Munich that Mr. Twades wrote to me. He was the new consulate general in Munich. So he wrote me asking what do I want, what do I want from him? And that he can't see me because he's doesn't have an office. He's a guest of American consul general.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if I write him what I want from him, he might answer. And I wrote him. I wrote him. I told him that it is in his interest, if he doesn't have an office, I am living in Steinberg. It's about 15 kilometers from Munich. Does he-- get a car and come to see me. And that is his interest to do it. Then I got the next letter from him that he got provisional office and with greatest pleasure, he will see me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I went to Munich and I saw him. And he told me that he didn't steal the car. It was done by Ms. Hollyworth-- that he knows that she got the car through Romania and, later, through London. And she sold the car in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape four of the interview with Emanuel Darin. It's the 18th of September, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
